title: Acute Abdomen
objectives: Develop systematic approach to acute abdominal pain
Identify surgical emergencies requiring immediate intervention
Correlate pain location with likely diagnoses

vignette.text:
A 65-year-old man presents with sudden onset severe abdominal pain that started 4 hours ago. The pain is diffuse and constant. He has vomited twice. He has history of atrial fibrillation and stopped his anticoagulation 2 weeks ago for a dental procedure.

Past Medical History: Atrial fibrillation, Hypertension, Type 2 DM
Medications: Metoprolol (stopped apixaban 2 weeks ago)

physicalExam.abdomen: Soft but diffusely tender, minimal guarding, bowel sounds present but hypoactive. Pain seems out of proportion to exam findings.

questions.question: The finding of 'pain out of proportion to exam' in a patient with AFib off anticoagulation suggests:
questions.explanation: Pain out of proportion to physical exam + atrial fibrillation + off anticoagulation = think mesenteric ischemia until proven otherwise. Arterial embolism to SMA is classic presentation.

questions.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging?
questions.explanation: CTA is the gold standard for mesenteric ischemia. Shows arterial occlusion and bowel wall changes. Time is critical - bowel becomes necrotic within hours.

questions.question: Which finding mandates emergent surgery?
questions.explanation: Free air (pneumoperitoneum) indicates perforated viscus - requires emergent laparotomy. Other causes of surgical abdomen: mesenteric ischemia with necrosis, ruptured AAA, bowel obstruction with strangulation.

keyTakeaways: Pain out of proportion to exam = think ischemia
Surgical emergencies: perforation, ischemia with necrosis, obstruction with strangulation
AFib + abdominal pain + off anticoagulation = mesenteric ischemia until proven otherwise

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected acute abdomen?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in acute abdomen. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of acute abdomen. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing acute abdomen, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

mcqs.stem: A 25-year-old male arrives after a motorcycle accident with distended neck veins, hypotension, and absent breath sounds on the left. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line, left side
options.explanation: This classic triad (JVD, hypotension, absent breath sounds) is tension pneumothorax — a clinical diagnosis requiring IMMEDIATE needle decompression before imaging. Per ATLS, insert a 14-gauge needle at the 2nd ICS midclavicular line (or 5th ICS anterior axillary line) followed by tube thoracostomy.

options.text: Obtain a portable chest X-ray to confirm the diagnosis
options.explanation: Tension pneumothorax is a CLINICAL diagnosis. Waiting for imaging delays life-saving treatment and can result in cardiovascular collapse and death. Treat first, image after.
While Beck's triad (JVD, hypotension, muffled heart sounds) suggests cardiac tamponade, the ABSENT BREATH SOUNDS distinguish tension pneumothorax. Tamponade does not cause absent breath sounds.

options.text: Intubate and start positive pressure ventilation
options.explanation: Positive pressure ventilation in an undecompressed tension pneumothorax will worsen the condition by forcing more air into the pleural space, accelerating cardiovascular collapse.
